summary: Would make a parent or guardian’s written declaration enough to obtain a religious exemption from K–12 vaccine or testing requirements and would require public higher education institutions to provide medical and religious exemptions from student vaccine or testing requirements

Adds American Academy of Pediatrics-recommended vaccines for children and adolescents age 18 and under to Alaska’s statewide immunization program and Medicaid-covered immunizations, alongside ACIP-recommended vaccines.

Standing order authorizes licensed Arizona healthcare professionals, including pharmacists and certain supervised pharmacy personnel, to assess eligibility and administer age-appropriate COVID-19 vaccines to people ages 6 months and older under ADHS protocol without a patient-specific prescription

Arizona's Medical Freedom Act, would broadly prohibit government entities, schools, businesses, and ticket issuers from denying employment, entry, services, or benefits based on whether a person has received or used a medical intervention, including vaccines and other medical treatments

Would require employers to provide a religious accommodation for employees who object to a medical product, expanding the current Arizona religious-exemption framework beyond the COVID-19 vaccination to a broader category of medical products

Authorizes specified providers to prescribe and administer certain vaccines based on California Department of Public Health recommendations rather than ACIP, updates state-regulated coverage and other vaccine-related requirements to use January 2025 federal recommendations as a floor with CDPH authority to modify or supplement them, and requires Medi-Cal to cover immunizations consistent with ACIP, AAP, ACOG, AAFP, and any CDPH updates, subject to federal approval.
Announces the West Coast Health Alliance will continue recommending AAP child and adolescent immunization schedule despite recent federal changes to childhood vaccine guidance
Directs the state Board of Health to consider recommendations from professional medical organizations (e.g., AAP, AAFP, etc.)

Codifies use of multiple scientific bodies (AAP, AAFP, etc.) alongside or instead of ACIP. Also expands provider authority, pharmacists can independently prescribe vaccines, updates insurance and coverage rules, strengthens liability protections. Includes adult immunization provisions and programmatic updates. Pending Governor's Signature/Veto

Confirms that state-regulated health plans must continue covering COVID-19 immunizations without cost sharing under Connecticut law based on recommendations from specified medical academies and state DPH guidance, even if ACIP changes its position

Authorizes the Public Health Commissioner to set state immunization standards for children and adults using recommendations from ACIP, AAP, AAFP, ACOG, ACP, IDSA, and the SMFM, require insurance coverage of those commissioner-recommended immunizations, and create a free adult flu-vaccine pilot program

Standing Order authorizes licensed pharmacists, and appropriately trained interns and certified pharmacy technicians under pharmacist supervision, to administer COVID-19 vaccines to eligible individuals based on AAP, AAFP, and ACOG recommendations rather than CDC guidance

Emergency bill allowing health providers to order and administer immunizations based not only on ACIP recommendations but also on schedules issued by another competent medical or public health organization designated by the D.C. Department of Health Director through public notice
Statement by the DC Department of Health Director designating AAP, ACOG, and AAFP as competent medical organizartions per A26-0149

Insurance bulletin requiring carriers to cover vaccines per ACIP recommendations as of December 31, 2024, including COVID-19 vaccines, and advising the Department may issue future guidance based on medical society recommendations.

Bill temporarily authorizes D.C. healthcare providers to order and administer immunizations recommended by competent medical or public health organizations designated by the D.C. Health Director, and also allows pharmacies to administer COVID-19 vaccines without a doctor’s prescription

Enacted but awaiting Congressional Review. Bill would authorize healthcare providers in the District to order and administer immunizations recommended by competent medical and public health organizations designated by the Director of the Department of Health.

Died in Messages on 3/13/26. Florida's Medical Freedom Act would impose new parental notice and acknowledgment requirements before minors receive vaccines and would expand school-entry immunization exemptions to include objections based on religion or conscience.
Emergency Proclamation enabling pharmacists to continue providing the COVID-19 vaccine to people 3 and older without a prescription; in effect through Nov. 22 2025
Insurance memorandum requiring continued coverage for COVID-19 vaccines even for off-label indications
Emergency Proclamation enabling pharmacists to continue providing the COVID-19 vaccine to people 3 and older without a prescription; in effect through Jan. 17 2026.
West Coast Health Alliance announces it recommends the American Academy of Pediatrics’ child and adolescent immunization schedule, despite recent federal changes

Hawaii proposed eliminating non-medical exemptions (e.g., religious exemptions) for school vaccine requirements in response to rising exemption rates, aiming to strengthen immunization compliance and public health protections.

Would require schools to provide a religious exemption from required immunizations and would bar a child from being subjected to medical examination, vaccination, revaccination, or immunization over a parent or guardian’s bona fide religious objection

Establishes a Hawaii Preventive Services Advisory Committee, authorizes DOH preventive-service recommendations, and requires certain state-regulated health coverage to cover recommended preventive services, including vaccines, without cost-sharing.

Legislation would repeal Idaho’s school and daycare immunization laws and replace them with a broader medical-mandates framework that bars public schools, daycares, and local governments from requiring a medical intervention, including vaccines, as a condition of access or participation

type_of_action: Decoupling / Reducing Reliance, Scope of Authority
summary: Standing Order authorizing healthcare professionals in pharmacies and other clinical settings to assess eligibility and administer age-appropriate COVID-19 vaccines without a patient-specific physician order to eligible people ages 6 months and older under IDPH’s protocol

Requires state-regulated insurers to continue covering IDPH-recommended vaccines, including COVID-19, MMR, Infuenza, and routine immunizations without interruption even if federal recommendations changed. This bulletin was superceded by HB 767

Legislation requires state-regulated health plans to cover immunizations recommended under State Guidelines for Communicable Disease Prevention issued by the Director of Public Health, and it creates an Immunization Advisory Committee to help inform those guidelines
Illinois's Immunziation Advisory Committee (formed per HB 767) adopts AAP's immunziation schedule

Standing Order authorizes healthcare professionals in pharmacies and other clinical settings to assess eligibility and administer age-appropriate COVID-19 vaccines without a patient-specific physician order to eligible people ages 6 months and older under IDPH’s protocol

Would expand conscience protections against required immunizations and more generally prohibit requiring a person to undergo a medical intervention as a condition of employment, admission, benefits, or participation
SB 304 aims to limit minors ability to seek vaccines for the prevention of HPV or Hep B unless they have parental consent.

Iowa's Medical Freedom Act would broadly bar businesses, gov't entities, and educational institutions from requiring or discriminating based on a person’s receipt of a medical intervention, including vaccines, while repealing Iowa statutes on child immunization requirements and certain COVID-19-specific provisions.
Kansas Department of Health & Environment's childhood immunization guidance links to recs from AAP and AAFP.

Directs that pharmacists may administer any FDA-authorized or licensed COVID-19 vaccine under a prescriber-approved protocol or prescription drug order even if ACIP has not updated its COVID-19 schedule, because Kentucky law independently authorizes that practice

Died due to inaction before the end of the legislative session. Would require employers that mandate immunization as a condition of employment to provide religious and medical exemptions, give written notice of those exemptions, and allow employees to sue over violations

Died due to inaction before the end of the legislative session. Remove hepatitis B from the list of required immunizations for children; prohibit additional immunizations from being required by the Cabinet for Health and Family Services without the approval of the General Assembly; delete requirement for hepatitis B immunization for sixth grade school attendance
HB 737 requires that certain students present immunization against meningococcal disease for school entry.

Standing Order authorizes qualified health care professionals to assess eligibility for and administer age-appropriate 2025–2026 COVID-19 vaccines under a statewide protocol based on evidence-based guidance from AAP, AAFP, and ACOG, without a patient-specific order at the time of care

Requires health insurance carriers to cover immunizations, including COVID-19 vaccines, when recommended by specified medical academies or otherwise required under Maine law, and to cover mandated COVID-19 vaccines and their administration without cost sharing or prior authorization

Maine eliminated religious and philosophical exemptions in 2019 (effective 2021), with existing exemptions grandfathered in until September 1, 2021. LD 174 (2025) proposed reinstating religious exemptions for school vaccines but did not pass.

Legislation expands the state’s Universal Childhood Immunization Program into a broader immunization program covering adults, updates how covered vaccines are selected and financed, and lets the Maine Vaccine Board procure and fund recommended vaccines for adults as well as children
Legislation expands pharmacist authority to prescribe, dispense, and administer FDA-approved vaccines and requires insurance coverage

Legislation requires the Maine Vaccine Board to consider vaccines recommended by the Northeast Public Health Collaborative when setting the Universal Immunization Program vaccine list, allows the board to request state funding if federal VFC funding is insufficient for certain vaccines, and provides liability protection for pharmacists administering vaccines under state or regional guidelines.
Updates insurance mandates (incl. state-regulated plans and Medicaid) to require no-cost-sharing coverage immunizations recommended by ACIP as of December 31, 2024
Altering the vaccines a pharmacist may administer to include ACIP recommendations as of December 31, 2024 or any new vaccines recommended since then.

Standing Order authorizing qualified health care professionals to assess eligibility for and administer age-appropriate COVID-19 vaccines under a statewide protocol based on AAP, ACOG, and AAFP guidance without a patient-specific order
Website update stating that Maryland recommends AAP's childhood vaccine schedule

Clinician letter advising providers to continue administering hepatitis B vaccine according to the standard of care, including recommendations from professional medical organizations such as AAP, AAFP, ACOG, and ACP, even if federal advisory recommendations change.

HB 637 requires the Maryland Secretary of Health to base recommendations for vaccines, screenings, and preventative services. It also retracted outdated language about the pertussis vaccine. Lastly, it expands scope of authority for pharmacists on certain vaccines they may administer, expanding access to preventative care.

SB 385 would require the Maryland Secretary of Health to issue state recommendations for immunizations, screenings, and preventive services, align pharmacist vaccine authority with those recommendations, and update required health insurance coverage to reflect those standards
Standing Order authorizes qualified pharmacy personnel at Massachusetts pharmacies to dispense and administer COVID-19 vaccine to eligible persons under a statewide protocol
Bulletin provides guidance that state-regulated insurers must continue covering recommended vaccines, including COVID-19 vaccines, consistent with state-directed vaccine policy
H 2554. No. A bill has been proposed where religious exemptions would be repealed and would not be allowed.

Legislation shifts state vaccine authority away from automatic dependence on ACIP by empowering the Commissioner of Public Health, with advice from a new immunization recommendations committee, to set alternative immunization standards needed to protect public health

Directive orders state agencies to take coordinated action to keep COVID-19 vaccines available, support provider and pharmacist administration, remove access barriers, and maintain coverage under state-regulated health plans

Recommendation treats any person over age 6 months who has not received a 2025–2026 COVID-19 vaccine as having a qualifying high-risk condition, thereby supporting eligibility for an age-appropriate dose of the updated COVID-19 vaccine

Bulletin advises that insurers in the individual and small group markets must continue covering COVID-19 vaccination without cost sharing and notes that even if ACIP changes its recommendation, any resulting coverage change would not take effect until the applicable future plan year under state law

Statement says the state will continue backing the full AAP and AAFP childhood immunization schedules despite federal changes to the CDC childhood schedule and says families should still be able to access those vaccines

Order directs state agencies to preserve vaccine access, reduce coverage and administration barriers, and develop state guidance, including a COVID-19 standing order or protocol, so Minnesotans can continue receiving vaccines despite federal changes

Standing Order authorizes licensed pharmacists and qualified delegated pharmacy personnel to assess eligibility for and administer age-appropriate 2025–2026 COVID-19 vaccines to people ages 12 through 64 under a non-patient-specific statewide protocol based on AAP, ACOG, AAFP, and IDSA recommendations
In 2025, SF 3439 was introduced to eliminate the option of opting out of vaccine requirements for measles, mumps, and rubella.

Press release announces Minnestota will now align all of its immunization guidance with professional medical association recommendations rather than CDC recommendations, using AAP for children/adolescents, AAFP for adults, and ACOG for pregnancy; it also says this extends Minnesota’s prior departure from CDC guidance on COVID-19 and hepatitis B to all vaccines
Mississippi State Dept of Health recommends universal Hep B birth dose and screening for mothers at birth
Would make it an unlawful discriminatory practice in employment and housing to treat someone differently because of that person’s private medical history or medical choices.

type_of_action: Decoupling / Reducing Reliance, Scope of Authority
summary: Reduces reliance on future ACIP recommendations for pharmacist-administered vaccines by replacing automatic incorporation of future CDC/ACIP recommendations with the federal standards in effect on January 1, 2025, unless modified by Nevada's State Board of Health
Health Alert to providers stating that theyn should refer to medical professional societies like AAP and ACOG.
Health Alert tell providers to continue following AAP and AAFP schedules as the state’s evidence-based guidance for routine childhood vaccines

Voted down 2/19/2026. Would prohibit schools, businesses, government entities, and other organizations from requiring or discriminating based on a person’s receipt of a medical intervention, including vaccines as part of that broader category
HB 1022 allows parents and guardians to sign an attestation rather than a standarized form, allowing parents to opt out of required school vaccines based on religious beliefs.
Would remove hepatitis B from the list of diseases for which immunization is required under state law

Directive mandates the Department of Health to preserve access to 2025–2026 COVID-19 vaccines by continuing to recommend vaccination, removing affordability and access barriers, and issuing a standing order so pharmacy personnel can administer authorized COVID-19 vaccines to eligible patients w/o prescription

type_of_action: Decoupling / Reducing Reliance, Scope of Authority
summary: Standing Order authorizes pharmacists and certain supervised pharmacy personnel to administer age-appropriate 2025–2026 FDA-approved COVID-19 vaccines to eligible patients in New Jersey under a non-patient-specific statewide order based on AAP, ACOG, and AAFP recommendations

New Jersey carriers are expected to continue covering COVID-19 vaccines and their administration without cost sharing, references DOH guidance recommending vaccination for people 6 months and older, and points to the DOH standing order authorizing pharmacy personnel to administer age-appropriate FDA-approved COVID-19 vaccines to eligible individuals

Directs health care providers to continue following AAP guidance by giving all newborns a hepatitis B vaccine dose within 24 hours of birth, requiring faster vaccination for higher-risk infants, ensuring children complete the full series, and rejecting ACIP’s December 2025 shift toward shared clinical decision-making for some newborns

Northeast Public Health Collaborative continues to recommend the American Academy of Pediatrics’ child and adolescent immunization schedule in response to recent federal changes to childhood vaccine guidance

Legislation makes the New Jersey Department of Health, rather than ACIP alone, the state’s primary source for immunization recommendations across insurance coverage, Medicaid, public employee health plans, and other vaccine-related statutes, while requiring DOH to consider federal and major medical society guidance

Would prohibit discrimination against people who have not received a COVID-19 vaccine by banning COVID-19 vaccine inquiries, mandates, and proof-of-vaccination requirements across employment, education, health care, business, travel, and other settings
Would prohibit public K-12 schools from requiring students to receive a COVID-19 vaccine as a condition of attendance

Directs the Department of Health to work with the Board of Pharmacy and other agencies to remove access barriers, expand pharmacy access, and issue public guidance for the 2025–2026 COVID-19 vaccine season

Authorizes pharmacists exercising prescriptive authority to prescribe and administer a broad range of vaccines using current evidence-based recommendations from ACIP, AAP, AAFP, ACOG, VIP, or the New Mexico Department of Health

Recommends broad access to 2025–2026 COVID-19 vaccines and tells providers to follow the AAP for pediatric vaccination, the ACOG for pregnant and recently pregnant people, and NMDOH’s own risk-based recommendations for adults

Directs carriers to continue covering immunizations during the 2025 and 2026 plan years based on the ACIP recommendations in place when rates were filed and approved, with any ACIP-driven coverage changes deferred until the 2027 plan year

Legislation shifts key vaccine authority toward the state by requiring the Department of Health to issue immunization recommendations for children and adults using guidance from ACIP, AAP, AAFP, ACOG, ACP, while also preserving school immunization requirements, strengthening the statewide childhood vaccine purchasing program, and requiring state-regulated plans to cover NMDOH-recommended immunizations without cost sharing
Governor and NMDOH statement advising the New Mexico will maintain vaccine schedule in the face of federal changes.

Declares a disaster emergency in response to federal actions threatening vaccine access and temporarily authorizes the state to take emergency steps to preserve vaccine availability, distribution, administration, and coverage

Reminds state-regulated insurers that they must continue covering in-network immunizations and their administration without cost sharing under New York law, including continued no-cost coverage of COVID-19 immunizations for children through age 19, and strongly encourages continued coverage of all immunizations recommended by ACIP as of September 10, 2025 even if ACIP later removes them

Northeast Public Health Collaborative continues to recommend the American Academy of Pediatrics’ child and adolescent immunization schedule in response to recent federal changes to childhood vaccine guidance

Bill would require children’s vaccine and immunization standards to be set by regulations issued by the commissioner using generally accepted medical standards and recommendations from AAP, AAFP, ACOG, ACP, ACIP, or similar recognized scientific organizations

Restores a religious exemption from school vaccination requirements, affecting immunizations required under state law for diseases including polio, measles, mumps, rubella, varicella, hepatitis B, pertussis, tetanus, Hib, meningococcal disease, and pneumococcal disease

type_of_action: Decoupling / Reducing Reliance, Scope of Authority
summary: Requires administration of certain vaccines for children in accordance with regulations issued by the commissioner, based on recommendations of AAP, AAFP, ACOG, the American College of Physicians, ACIP, and/or other similar nationally or internationally recognized scientific organizations; removes certain requirements for vaccines to be administered under federal guidance.

RESCINDED. Originally directed North Carolina’s State Health Director to issue a statewide standing order to facilitate access to FDA-approved COVID-19 vaccines, notwithstanding any contrary ACIP action, so long as the order remains consistent with FDA approvals and the PREP Act

Announces that the state does not expect to change its childhood immunization clinical guidance despite revised CDC schedules and notes that evidence-based recommendations from the AAP remain unchanged while previously recommended vaccines stay accessible and covered

Directs major state health and human services agencies to comprehensively review and revise policies related to medical freedom, informed consent, and parental rights, including vaccine-related practices, in light of the administration’s concerns about COVID-19 mandates and the federal childhood immunization schedule

Oklahoma's Medical Freedom Act would bar businesses, schools, ticket issuers, and public entities from discriminating against people based on whether they have received or used a medical intervention, including vaccines as part of that broader category

Announced adoption of the West Coast Health Alliance respiratory vaccine recommendations to preserve access to COVID-19, influenza, and RSV vaccines, maintain pharmacy and provider administration authority, and ensure coverage continuity ahead of respiratory virus season

Directs all Oregon health benefit plans to continue covering FDA-approved COVID-19 vaccines and their administration without cost sharing or network restrictions, consistent with prior state requirements and the state’s broader vaccine-access approach
Announces the West Coast Health Alliance will continue recommending AAP child and adolescent immunization schedule despite recent federal changes to childhood vaccine guidance

Directs state agencies to preserve access to vaccines and align state guidance, Medicaid, insurance coverage, and school-related vaccine policy with evidence-based recommendations from AAP, AAFP, and ACOG

type_of_action: Scope of Authority, Decoupling / Reducing Reliance
summary: Approved ACOG, AAP, and AAFP as competent authorities for pharmacist-administered immunizations, and approved the FDA as a competent authority for COVID-19 immunizations, allowing pharmacists and pharmacy interns to continue vaccinating under those guidelines in addition to ACIP

Directs insurers to continue covering, without cost sharing or other barriers, the vaccines promised in policies approved for sale through 2026, including vaccines administered under guidance from ACIP, ACOG, AAP, AAFP, and, for COVID-19 immunizations, the FDA

Would require non-grandfathered health plans to cover, without cost sharing, immunizations covered in Pennsylvania as of January 1, 2025, while allowing the state to add or remove covered immunizations based on evidence and reports from AAP, ACOG, ACP, AAFP, the IDSA, and the SMFM.
States that federal changes do not alter Pennsylvania children’s vaccine access or recommendations and that the state will continue following AAP immunization schedule for children
Authorizes pharmacists to adminsiter COVID-19 vaccines off-label consistent with AAP, AAFP, ACOG, and ACP.

Directs Rhode Island-licensed health care entities to cover 2025–2026 COVID-19 vaccines and their administration without cost sharing in accordance with RIDOH guidance and recommendations from AAP, AAFP, ACOG, and ACP

Advises clinicians to continue giving all newborns a hepatitis B vaccine birth dose within 24 hours and to complete the pediatric series in line with AAP schedule, while also referencing prenatal hepatitis B screening recommendations from ACOG

Northeast Public Health Collaborative continues to recommend the American Academy of Pediatrics’ child and adolescent immunization schedule in response to recent federal changes to childhood vaccine guidance
The state of South Carolina has introducted a senate bill to remove religious exemption for the MMR vaccine.

Authorizes qualified pharmacists, pharmacy interns, and certain pharmacy technicians under supervision to assess vaccine needs and administer covered vaccines under statewide rules based on recommendations from ACIP, AAP, AAFP, and ACOG

Lost House floor vote on 2/20/2026. Legislation would prohibit employers, educational institutions, state agencies, political subdivisions, and other persons from requiring an individual to receive a COVID-19 vaccination as a condition of employment, enrollment, or receiving a benefit or service

Authorizes qualified pharmacists, pharmacy interns, and pharmacy technicians to administer age-appropriate updated COVID-19 vaccines to people age 5 and older under state recommendations supported by AAP, ACOG, AAFP, IDSA

Advises providers to continue recommending and offering 2025–2026 COVID-19, influenza, and RSV immunization based on guidance from the CDC, AAP, ACOG, AAFP, and IDSA, and notes that Vermont had issued a pharmacy standing order the day before for COVID-19 vaccination

Directs Vermont-regulated health plans to cover vaccines recommended by the Vermont Department of Health and ACIP, including COVID-19, influenza, and RSV vaccines, without cost sharing or utilization review when members meet Vermont guidance

Northeast Public Health Collaborative continues to recommend the American Academy of Pediatrics’ child and adolescent immunization schedule in response to recent federal changes to childhood vaccine guidance

Authorizes qualified pharmacists and pharmacy interns to dispense and administer 2025–2026 FDA-approved COVID-19 vaccines to eligible adults age 18 and older under a statewide protocol grounded in FDA-authorized product criteria

Recommends current COVID-19 vaccination for everyone 6 months and older, including pregnant people, based on advice from the Washington State Vaccine Advisory Committee, and explains that Apple Health continues to cover the vaccine while private-plan coverage depends on the patient’s specific insurer
Authorizes qualified health care providers to administer updated COVID-19 vaccines to people age 6 months and older, including pregnant individuals, using guidance from AAP, ACOG, and AAFP
Announces the West Coast Health Alliance will continue recommending AAP child and adolescent immunization schedule despite recent federal changes to childhood vaccine guidance
Died due to inaction before the end of the legislative session. SB26 would allow religious and philosophical beliefs as an exemption for childhood vaccines for parents and guardians.

Directs state agencies to preserve vaccine access by coordinating on coverage, provider authority, public guidance, and possible standing orders for COVID-19 and other routine vaccines, drawing on recommendations from AAFP, AAP, and ACOG
Authorizes qualified health care personnel to vaccinate people ages 6 months and older without a patient-specific prescription based on recommendations from AAP, ACOG, the AAFP, and the WHO

Directs insurers and governmental self-funded plans are expected to cover COVID-19 vaccinations without cost sharing for all policyholders, relying on Governor Evers’ Executive Order 275 and the state standing medical order
A bill introduced in January 2026 wanted to eliminate the personal exemption belief for vaccines. This bill has failed to pass in March 2026.

Adivses clinicians that the state will not change its childhood vaccine recommendations in response to recent federal schedule changes and will continue recommending the full AAP childhood immunization schedule
